content.text: What is the Azure Developer CLI (azd)?

The Azure Developer CLI (azd) is a higher-level developer tool that wraps the Azure CLI and Bicep/Terraform to provide an application-centric workflow. Where the Azure CLI operates at the resource level ('create this storage account'), azd operates at the application level ('set up everything this app needs and deploy it').

azd is built around a standardized project template structure (azure.yaml + infra/ folder) that describes both the application's infrastructure and how to build and deploy the app code. Once that structure is in place, a single 'azd up' command provisions all infrastructure and deploys the application.

attrs.alt: azd application lifecycle: init → auth → provision → deploy; azd up combines provision and deploy; azd pipeline config generates CI/CD

content.text:
# Windows (winget)
winget install microsoft.azd

# macOS (Homebrew)
brew tap azure/azd && brew install azd

# Linux (curl script)
curl -fsSL https://aka.ms/install-azd.sh | bash

# npm (all platforms)
npm install -g @azure/dev

# VS Code extension
code --install-extension ms-azuretools.azure-dev

# Verify installation
azd version
Core Concepts: azure.yaml and the infra/ Folder
Every azd project has two defining pieces: azure.yaml (the app manifest) and an infra/ folder (the infrastructure definition). Understanding these files is key to understanding how azd works.

# azure.yaml — azd application manifest
name: my-web-app

# Services describe the deployable components of the application
services:
  web:
    project: ./src/web         # path to the service source code
    language: js               # js, ts, python, csharp, java, go
    host: appservice           # appservice, containerapp, function, aks

  api:
    project: ./src/api
    language: python
    host: containerapp
    docker:
      path: ./src/api/Dockerfile  # override default build

# Optional: hooks run before/after azd commands
hooks:
  preprovision:
    shell: sh
    run: scripts/pre-provision.sh
  postdeploy:
    shell: sh
    run: scripts/post-deploy.sh
The infra/ folder contains Bicep or Terraform files that describe the Azure resources needed by the application. azd reads this folder during 'azd provision'.

# Typical azd project structure
my-app/
├── azure.yaml                # azd manifest
├── infra/
│   ├── main.bicep            # top-level Bicep template
│   ├── main.parameters.json  # parameter defaults
│   └── modules/
│       ├── appservice.bicep  # App Service + plan
│       ├── cosmos.bicep      # Cosmos DB account
│       └── keyvault.bicep    # Key Vault
├── src/
│   └── web/                  # application code
│       ├── package.json
│       └── src/
└── .azure/
    └── dev/                  # azd environment state (auto-created)
        └── .env              # environment variables set by azd
A typical azd session follows four commands: init, auth, provision, deploy (or the combined 'up').
azd init: Start from a Template

# Initialize from an Azure template gallery entry
azd init --template todo-nodejs-mongo

# Initialize from a GitHub repo
azd init --template https://github.com/Azure-Samples/todo-python-mongo-aca

# Initialize in an existing project (azd detects your stack)
cd my-existing-project
azd init
# azd analyzes the codebase and suggests a template structure

# Sign in interactively (browser opens)
azd auth login

# Sign in for CI/CD (service principal)
azd auth login \
  --client-id $AZURE_CLIENT_ID \
  --client-secret $AZURE_CLIENT_SECRET \
  --tenant-id $AZURE_TENANT_ID

# Show current auth state
azd auth show

# Create a new environment (dev, staging, prod)
azd env new dev
azd env new staging

# Select the active environment
azd env select dev

# List environments
azd env list

# Set environment-specific variables
azd env set AZURE_LOCATION eastus
azd env set AZURE_SUBSCRIPTION_ID 'your-sub-id'
azd env set MY_APP_SETTING 'custom-value'

# Environment variables are stored in .azure/<env>/.env
# and injected into infrastructure parameters and app settings automatically

# Provision all infrastructure defined in infra/ folder
azd provision

# Preview what will be created (no changes)
azd provision --preview

# Provision with a specific environment
azd provision --environment staging

# After provisioning, azd writes outputs to .azure/<env>/.env:
# AZURE_RESOURCE_GROUP=myapp-dev-abc123
# WEB_URI=https://myapp-web.azurewebsites.net
# COSMOS_CONNECTION_STRING=mongodb://...
azd deploy: Build and Deploy App Code

# Deploy all services defined in azure.yaml
azd deploy

# Deploy a specific service only
azd deploy --service web
azd deploy --service api

# azd deploy does:
# 1. Runs the language-appropriate build (npm run build, pip install, dotnet publish)
# 2. Packages the output
# 3. Pushes to the host (App Service Zip deploy, ACR + Container App update, etc.)
# 4. Streams deployment logs

# Most common command: provision infra + deploy app in one step
azd up

# azd up is idempotent:
# - If infrastructure already exists, it updates it (Bicep incremental mode)
# - If the app is already deployed, it redeploys it
# - Safe to run on every git push

# Target a specific environment:
azd up --environment prod

# Run without prompts (CI mode):
azd up --no-prompt
Microsoft and the community publish hundreds of azd-compatible templates in the Azure Developer CLI template gallery. These are complete, working examples that provision real Azure infrastructure.

# Browse available templates
azd template list

# Search for templates
azd template list --filter 'nodejs'
azd template list --filter 'python containerapp'

# Common starter templates:
# todo-nodejs-mongo          → Node.js + Cosmos DB on App Service
# todo-python-mongo-aca      → Python + Cosmos DB on Container Apps
# azure-functions-nodejs-openai → Node.js Functions + OpenAI
# aks-store-demo             → Multi-service app on AKS

# Initialize from the gallery:
azd init --template todo-python-mongo-aca
azd up   # provisions and deploys the full template in minutes
App Service, Cosmos DB (Mongo API), Key Vault
Container Apps, Cosmos DB, Container Registry
App Service, OpenAI, Cognitive Search, Storage
Once your application works locally with azd, you can generate a CI/CD pipeline that runs azd up automatically on every push to main.

# Generate a GitHub Actions workflow that runs azd up on push
azd pipeline config --provider github

# This command:
# 1. Creates a service principal with the right RBAC roles
# 2. Stores credentials as GitHub Actions secrets
# 3. Generates .github/workflows/azure-dev.yml

# Generate an Azure DevOps pipeline instead
azd pipeline config --provider azdo

# View the generated workflow
cat .github/workflows/azure-dev.yml

# Generated .github/workflows/azure-dev.yml
name: Azure Developer CLI

on:
  workflow_dispatch:
  push:
    branches:
      - main

jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Checkout
        uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Install azd
        uses: Azure/setup-azd@v1

      - name: Log in with Azure (Federated Credentials)
        run: |
          azd auth login \
            --client-id '${{ vars.AZURE_CLIENT_ID }}' \
            --federated-credential-provider 'github' \
            --tenant-id '${{ vars.AZURE_TENANT_ID }}'

      - name: Provision Infrastructure
        run: azd provision --no-prompt
        env:
          AZURE_ENV_NAME: ${{ vars.AZURE_ENV_NAME }}
          AZURE_LOCATION: ${{ vars.AZURE_LOCATION }}
          AZURE_SUBSCRIPTION_ID: ${{ vars.AZURE_SUBSCRIPTION_ID }}

      - name: Deploy Application
        run: azd deploy --no-prompt
        env:
          AZURE_ENV_NAME: ${{ vars.AZURE_ENV_NAME }}
azd vs az CLI: When to Use Each
Scaffold a new app + infrastructure together
Templates wire app and infra together
Handles build, push, and infra in one step
Generate CI/CD pipeline with OIDC auth
Handles SP creation and secret wiring automatically
az CLI output is easier to script
Deletes the resource group azd created, safely
az CLI (az keyvault, az webapp config)
azd hooks let you run custom scripts at key points in the workflow: before or after provision, deploy, or up. This is where you add database migrations, seed data, or post-deployment health checks.

# azure.yaml with hooks
name: my-app

services:
  api:
    project: ./src/api
    language: python
    host: containerapp

hooks:
  # Runs before infrastructure is provisioned
  preprovision:
    shell: sh
    run: |
      echo 'Validating environment variables...'
      [ -n "$AZURE_SUBSCRIPTION_ID" ] || { echo 'Missing AZURE_SUBSCRIPTION_ID'; exit 1; }

  # Runs after infrastructure is provisioned (before deploy)
  postprovision:
    shell: sh
    run: scripts/seed-keyvault.sh   # store secrets in Key Vault

  # Runs after all services are deployed
  postdeploy:
    shell: sh
    run: |
      echo 'Running smoke tests...'
      curl -f ${WEB_URI}/health || { echo 'Health check failed'; exit 1; }

# Delete all resources provisioned by azd (asks for confirmation)
azd down

# Skip confirmation prompt (for CI or scripting)
azd down --force

# Also purge soft-deleted resources (Key Vault, Cognitive Services)
azd down --purge

# Tear down a specific environment
azd down --environment staging

# azd down deletes the resource group that azd created.
# If you provisioned into an existing resource group, use:
# az group delete --name <rg> --yes

azd down only deletes resources that azd provisioned (tracked in the azd environment state). If you created additional resources manually in the same resource group, they will also be deleted since azd deletes the entire resource group.

Each azd environment maps to a distinct deployment target — dev, staging, production — and stores its own configuration values in a .env file under .azure/<env-name>/.env. Environment values are injected into Bicep/Terraform as parameters and into the app as environment variables.

# Create a new named environment
azd env new staging

# List all environments
azd env list

# Select an active environment
azd env select staging

# Show current environment values
azd env get-values

# Store a database connection string (stored in .azure/staging/.env)
azd env set DATABASE_URL "Server=myserver.database.windows.net;Database=mydb"

# Store a secret by referencing an existing Key Vault secret
azd env set --keyvault mykeyvault API_KEY "@Microsoft.KeyVault(SecretUri=https://mykeyvault.vault.azure.net/secrets/api-key/)"

# Read a specific value
azd env get-values | grep DATABASE_URL

# Refresh values after manual changes in Azure Portal
azd env refresh

When azd provisions infrastructure, output values from Bicep/Terraform (like resource names, connection strings, endpoints) are automatically captured and stored in the active environment's .env file.

// main.bicep — outputs are captured by azd into .azure/<env>/.env
output AZURE_STORAGE_ACCOUNT_NAME string = storageAccount.name
output AZURE_COSMOS_DB_CONNECTION_STRING string = cosmosAccount.listConnectionStrings().connectionStrings[0].connectionString
output SERVICE_API_URI string = appService.properties.defaultHostName

The azure.yaml file supports multi-service projects where each service has its own source path, build configuration, and deployment target. azd orchestrates builds and deployments in dependency order.

# azure.yaml — multi-service project
name: my-ecommerce-app
services:
  api:
    project: ./src/api         # Python FastAPI
    language: py
    host: appservice
    module: ./infra/api

  web:
    project: ./src/web         # React SPA
    language: ts
    host: staticwebapp
    module: ./infra/web

  worker:
    project: ./src/worker      # .NET background service
    language: dotnet
    host: containerapp
    module: ./infra/worker

pipeline:
  provider: github

# Deploy only the API service (skip others)
azd deploy api

# Deploy web and worker but not api
azd deploy web worker

# Build without deploying (useful for image caching in CI)
azd package api --output-path ./dist/api.zip

# Stream logs from a running service
azd monitor --logs --service api
azd supports Terraform as an alternative to Bicep for the infra/ layer. Set the platform in azure.yaml and ensure infra/main.tf exists. azd wraps terraform init, plan, and apply behind azd provision.

# azure.yaml — Terraform backend
name: my-terraform-app
infra:
  provider: terraform
  path: infra

services:
  api:
    project: ./src/api
    language: py
    host: appservice
azd injects AZURE_ENV_NAME and AZURE_LOCATION as Terraform input variables automatically. Outputs prefixed with AZURE_ or SERVICE_ are captured into the environment's .env file.
azd integrates with Azure Monitor to surface logs and metrics without leaving the terminal. The azd monitor command opens Application Insights dashboards or streams structured logs.

# Open Application Insights Live Metrics in the browser
azd monitor --overview

# Stream real-time logs to the terminal
azd monitor --logs

# Stream logs for a specific service only
azd monitor --logs --service api

# Open the Azure Portal resource group view
azd monitor

# Show deployment operation details (useful when azd provision fails)
azd provision --debug 2>&1 | tee provision-debug.log

# Check ARM deployment history for the environment
az deployment group list   --resource-group rg-$(azd env get-values | grep AZURE_RESOURCE_GROUP | cut -d= -f2)   --query "[].{Name:name, State:properties.provisioningState, Time:properties.timestamp}"   --output table

# Pull the specific failed operation
az deployment operation group list   --resource-group rg-myenv   --name azd-provision   --query "[?properties.provisioningState=='Failed'].properties.statusMessage"   -o json
Bicep module paths in azure.yaml don't match infra/ structure
Check module: values in azure.yaml match actual .bicep file paths
azd deploy succeeds but app returns 500
Environment variables not injected — azd outputs not in .env
Run azd env refresh then azd deploy again
Run azd env select <name> or azd env new <name>
Pipeline config fails auth in GitHub Actions
Service principal lacks Contributor on subscription
Re-run azd pipeline config after granting SP permissions
azd init defaults to latest release tag
Use azd init --template owner/repo@branch to pin
Resources created outside Bicep/Terraform not tracked
Delete manually via az or Azure Portal
Run azd env get-values before debugging any deployment — it shows all injected values and reveals missing outputs that are causing runtime failures.

The Azure Developer CLI (azd) is an application-centric tool that standardizes the full lifecycle of cloud application development: scaffold (azd init), authenticate (azd auth login), provision infrastructure (azd provision), deploy app code (azd deploy), or do everything at once (azd up). Projects are defined by azure.yaml (service definitions and hooks) and an infra/ folder (Bicep or Terraform). azd supports multiple named environments (dev, staging, prod), each with independent state stored in .azure/<env>/.env. The 'azd pipeline config' command generates a complete GitHub Actions or Azure DevOps pipeline with OIDC-based authentication. Use azd for greenfield application development and end-to-end environment management; use the az CLI for fine-grained, resource-level operations on existing infrastructure.
